title: Acquisition Channel Decay

content.statement: All channels get worse over time.
content.question: Which of your business's acquisition channels is already decaying? What's your next channel bet?
sop: Track cost-per-customer (or cost-per-lead) monthly for each acquisition channel, not just spend and revenue totals.
Calculate the percentage change in efficiency for each channel over the last 6 months (see calculation, page 5).
Flag any channel down more than 20% in efficiency as decaying and set a review date.
Test one new channel at roughly 10% of budget every quarter, regardless of how well your current channel is performing.
Never let one channel exceed 60% of total acquisition, build the backup before you need it.
content.explainer: Every channel you love today will decay. Always be testing the next one.
questions: Which of your business's acquisition channels is already decaying? What's your next channel bet?
If your best channel died tomorrow, how many days of revenue could you survive before the next one kicked in?
What's the earliest warning sign your top channel is decaying, rising cost per lead, or you just haven't checked in months?
content.equation_example: Initial Efficiency 10 customers per $1,000 spent, Current Efficiency 6 customers per $1,000 spent, Time 4 months. (10 minus 6) divided by 4 equals 1.0 point of efficiency lost per month. Higher or rising means the channel is decaying fast and needs replacing soon. Lower or near-zero means the channel is still healthy.
content.closing_statement: Treat every channel as temporary, not core infrastructure.

caption:
Principle Nº 91: Acquisition Channel Decay

All channels get worse over time. Every channel you love today will decay.

Ask yourself: Which of your business's acquisition channels is already decaying? What's your next channel bet?
